concepts.title: Sucrose
concepts.conceptBody:
Disaccharides

A disaccharide upon hydrolysis liberates two monosaccharide units. These two molecules are held together by a glycosidic bond. The monosaccharides liberated due to hydrolysis may be of similar or different molecules. The disaccharides are of two types. They are - Reducing sugars and nonreducing sugars.

The most common disaccharides are:

  • Sucrose (cane sugar) made up of glucose + fructose
  • Maltose (Malt sugar) made up of glucose + glucose

Sucrose

Sucrose is made up of $\alpha $-D-Glucose and $\beta $-D-fructose held together by a glycosidic bond, between ${{\text{C}}_{1}}$ of $\alpha $-glucose and ${{\text{C}}_{2}}$ of $\beta $-fructose. The reducing groups of glucose and fructose are involved in glycosidic bond, so it is a non-reducing sugar.

Sucrose is a colourless, crystalline and sweet substance soluble in water. Sucrase is the enzyme that can hydrolyze sucrose in the body.

Sucrose is a non-reducing sugar as carbony1 group is not free

(i) Inversion of cane sugar On hydrolysis with dilute acids or enzyme invertose, canesugar gives equimolar mixture of $D(+)$ glucose and $D(-)$ fructose.

$\underset{{{[\alpha ]}_{D}}=+66.5}{\mathop{\underset{\text{Sucrose}}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{12}}{{\text{H}}_{22}}{{\text{O}}_{11}}+}}\,}}\,{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{\text{HCl}}\underset{{}}{\mathop{\underset{{}}{\mathop{\underset{{{[\alpha ]}_{D}}=+52.5}{\mathop{\underset{\text{D - Glucose}}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{12}}{{\text{O}}_{6}}}}\,}}\,+\underset{{{[\alpha ]}_{D}}=-92.4}{\mathop{\underset{\text{D fructose}}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}_{12}}{{\text{O}}_{6}}}}\,}}\,}}\,}}\,$

Sucrose is dextrorotatory but after hydrolysis gives dextrorotary glucose and laevorotatory fructose. Since the laevorotation of fructose $(-92.4)$ is more than dextrotation of glucose $(+52.5)$ the mixture is laevorotatory. Thus hydrolysis of sucrose brings a change in the sign of rotation from dextro(+) is laevo(-) and is known as inversion and the mixture is known as invert sugar.

Sucrose solution is fermented by yeast when the enzyme invertase hydrolyses sucrose to glucose and fructose.

$\underset{\text{ Sucrose }}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{12}}{{\text{H}}_{22}}}}\,{{\text{O}}_{11}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{\text{ Invertase}}\underset{\text{ glucose }}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{12}}}}\,{{\text{O}}_{6}}+\underset{\text{ fructose }}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{12}}{{\text{O}}_{6}}}}\,$

Enzyme zymase converts these monosaccarides to ethyl alcohol.

$\underset{monosaccaride}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{12}}{{\text{O}}_{6}}}}\,\xrightarrow{\text{zymese}}2{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{OH}+2\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

concepts.title: Maltose
concepts.conceptBody: (ii) Maltose: Another disaccharide, maltose is composed of two$\langle \alpha $-D-glucose units in which$\text{C}1$of one glucose (I) is linked to$\text{C}4$of another glucose unit (II). The free aldehyde group can be produced at$\text{C}1$of second glucose in solution and it shows reducing properties so it is a reducing sugar.
